source: On The Tape / RiskReversal — Fear is the market killer
summary: Commodity inflation is the newest and most geopolitically-driven inflation component; capitalism will solve it but more slowly
summary_extended: Baker identifies commodity inflation as a distinct third wave of inflation, separate from goods/services inflation and supply chain disruptions, which was dramatically accelerated by the Russia-Ukraine conflict. He argues that while capitalism is effective at solving commodity supply shortfalls through increased production and substitution, the process is inherently slower than resolving supply chain bottlenecks, meaning commodity-driven inflation will be more persistent. Baker expresses skepticism that deglobalization will be permanent but acknowledges it as a massive and highly uncertain variable that could structurally alter commodity markets and supply chains. He offers no specific investment vehicle or ticker as a direct implication, framing this as a macro backdrop observation rather than a stock-specific thesis. The investment implication is broadly that commodity inflation will remain elevated for longer than consensus expects, but Baker stops short of naming specific commodity producers or ETFs as actionable ideas.
detail: Baker identifies commodity inflation as a third, newer component of inflation that was accelerated into hyperdrive by Russia-Ukraine. He notes capitalism can solve commodity supply problems but it takes longer than supply chain fixes. He is skeptical of permanent deglobalization but acknowledges it as a massive variable.
confidence_evidence: Baker says capitalism is 'very good at solving these problems. Just takes longer than solving a supply chain problem.' He is explicitly uncertain about the geopolitical trajectory.
quote_fragment: capitalism is also very good at solving these problems. Just takes longer
